nativeRange.habitat: Sheltered tropical reefs at 3–20 m depth. Lives in close association with long-spined sea urchins (Diadema spp.) or branching corals, using the host for protection and as a foraging substrate.

decorPreferences: branching corals (Acropora, Stylophora) as host substrate
long-spine sea urchin (Diadema spp.) as host substrate

diet.notes: In the wild, juveniles graze on pedicellariae and tube feet of their Diadema urchin host plus copepods; adults specialize on small burrowing bivalves embedded in corals and on urchin tube feet. In captivity, generally takes finely chopped fresh or frozen marine fish, squid, mysis shrimp, and vitamin-enriched brine shrimp once feeding is established. Initial acceptance of prepared foods is often the largest husbandry hurdle — live foods or targeted feeding near the host structure may be needed to initiate feeding.

avoidWith: fish that compete for small meaty foods

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 2036x1527. Tack-sharp single adult against a glowing red Diadema urchin host, showing the diagnostic maroon body, bright yellow-white lateral stripe and needle snout; striking and diagnostic of the urchin association.
Rickard Zerpe; 1684x1263. Sharp reef shot showing TWO individuals, illustrating how the species is kept as a pair; clear stripe, snout and coloration on both fish.
portioid; ~1024px. Clean single-specimen shot against open blue water with whole body incl. yellow-marked tail in frame; sharp and vibrant, different photographer/composition for variety.

summary: The Yellow Stripe Clingfish is a small Indo-Pacific specialist that lives in close association with long-spine sea urchins or branching corals, reaching around 5 cm. Getting new arrivals to accept prepared foods is often the main challenge, making this a species best suited to experienced reef keepers.
careNotes:
The Yellow Stripe Clingfish, also widely traded as the Urchin Clingfish, belongs to the family Gobiesocidae and has evolved a close commensal relationship with long-spine sea urchins (Diadema spp.) and branching SPS corals in the wild. The host provides both physical shelter and a foraging substrate — juveniles tend to graze the urchin's pedicellariae (small pincer-like defensive organs on the urchin's surface) and tube feet, while adults tend to specialize on small burrowing bivalves embedded in the bases of branching corals. This feeding habit has given them a reputation in reef-keeping circles as useful allies against pyramidellid-style parasitic snails and bivalves that can damage coral tissue.

The two main husbandry challenges are feeding and escape. New arrivals are commonly reported to refuse prepared foods, and starvation is frequently cited as the most common cause of early loss in this species. Offering live mysis, finely chopped fresh marine flesh, or vitamin-enriched frozen foods near the host structure is generally recommended to initiate feeding; once established on a routine, most individuals will accept prepared foods over time. On escape risk: this species has been reported to jump from open-top aquariums, so a tight-fitting lid is generally advised.

The species is generally regarded as peaceful toward other fish and reef invertebrates, but conspecific aggression has been reported — it is best kept singly, as a confirmed mated pair, or with no more than one specimen per host structure. Females have been reported to have longer, thinner snouts than males, which can help with pairing.

The Yellow Stripe Clingfish is widely considered reef-safe and is regarded as beneficial in some reef setups for its tendency to pick at parasitic bivalves on coral.
breedingNotes: Breeding has been reported in home reef aquariums by experienced hobbyists, though it is uncommon. Pair formation appears to require introducing a known male and female (distinguishable by snout length), and the eggs are demersal and laid on a substrate near the host. Sustained captive rearing of fry past first feeding is not widely documented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Direct species product page for Yellow Stripe Clingfish. Provided care level (Difficult), max size (2 in), min tank (10 gal), temperament (Peaceful), reef safe (Yes), diet (carnivore + notes), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), specific gravity (1.020–1.025), dKH (8–12), and origin (Indonesia). Note: original task URL https://www.liveaquaria.com/collections/gobies did not list this species (clingfish are Gobiesocidae, not Gobiidae); swapped to the species-specific product page.

additional.name: theaquariumwiki.com
additional.notes: Designated secondary returned HTTP 403 / access-denial on fetch; swapped per playbook to BlueZoo Aquatics, Quality Marine, and FishBase for cross-verification.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: First successful secondary. Confirmed scientific name, common names, family-adjacent grouping, max size (~2 in), reef compatibility (Yes), diet (carnivore). Diverged on tank size (24 US gal vs LA's 10 gal), temperament (Semi-aggressive vs LA's Peaceful), and care level (Intermediate vs LA's Difficult); native range listed as Solomon Islands (narrower than FishBase Indo-Pacific range).

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Third source. Family confirmed Gobiesocidae. Provided full water parameters matching LiveAquaria (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, 8–12 dKH). Tank size 20 US gal; care level Moderate; temperament generally peaceful with conspecific aggression noted. Documented sexual dimorphism (females have longer thinner snouts), urchin association, and jumping/lid requirement.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy and wild-range cross-check. Confirmed family Gobiesocidae and order Gobiesociformes; max length 5.0 cm TL; depth 3–20 m; Indo-Pacific range from Western Indian Ocean (Oman, Mauritius) east to Fiji/Tonga and north to southern Japan. Wild temperature 24.7–29.2°C (warmer than retail recommendations). Ecology confirmed: juveniles graze Diadema pedicellariae/sphaeridia and copepods; adults take burrowing bivalves in corals plus urchin tube feet.

fish.breedingNotes: Pair formation has been reported in advanced reef aquariums, with eggs laid on a substrate near the host structure. Rearing fry past their first feeding has not been commonly documented in captivity.
